title: HOW TO: Connecting Claude to Tradingview
author: DAlphaOne
selftext:
# Preface

This is not a perfect one-stop solution for all. This was something I did myself around my own computer and requirements. I also spent way too much time fighting the windows MSIX bs.

  
I have a Clawdbot (his name is Nibbles), who I chat with in our discord server. He runs on Sonnet 4.6 (cheap) via openrouter. I wanted him to be able to access charts, be able to actually write/execute pinescript, and more. 

I dont know the full capabilities yet, but heres how far I have gotten and a quick writeup on the Tradingview-MCP I found recently **(this is not mine).**

# Now Nibbles (running on Sonnet 4.6 via OpenRouter) has live eyes on my actual TradingView Desktop. He can:

* See the current symbol, timeframe, and price in real time
* Read indicator values and drawn levels
* Take screenshots of my chart
* Run and debug Pine Script with me
* Change symbols/timeframes, draw lines, manage alerts, and more

It’s not perfect (TradingView Desktop is a locked-down MSIX app, so launching with the remote debugging port is a pain), but it actually **works**.

# Quick Overview – TradingView MCP Setup (Windows)

I’m using this open-source project:  
[https://github.com/tradesdontlie/tradingview-mcp](https://github.com/tradesdontlie/tradingview-mcp)

It connects any MCP-compatible AI (Claude Desktop, my custom Clawbot, etc.) to TradingView via Chrome DevTools Protocol.

**What it can do right now:**

* Live chart reading
* Screenshot generation
* Pine Script assistance
* Symbol/timeframe control
* OHLCV data, quotes, etc.

# The Hard Part (and the fix I actually got working)

The official TradingView Desktop only installs as an **MSIX** app now (no more clean .exe). Normal launch scripts fail because Windows strips command-line flags.

I had to use the **IApplicationActivationManager COM API** to force-launch it with --remote-debugging-port=9222.

If you’re on Windows 10/11 and want to do the same, here’s the full write-up I made:

[https://astrofund.io/how-to-connecting-claude-clawdbot-to-tradingview/](https://astrofund.io/how-to-connecting-claude-clawdbot-to-tradingview/)

# Proof It Works

* node src/cli/index.js status → shows live chart data
* node src/cli/index.js screenshot → actually grabs my chart
* My bot can now say “Hey, on the 15m ES chart you have a clear bullish divergence on RSI…” with a fresh screenshot attached.

# Want to try it?

This is **not** a one-click magic button, it requires Node.js, Git, and some PowerShell wizardry. But if you’re technical and want your AI to stop guessing what the chart looks like, this is currently one of the better solutions.

I’ll be improving it and adding more tools over time (Nibbles is already calling the launch script himself when needed).

comments.author: meowflying
comments.body: Thank you very much for the workaround for Tradingview on  Windows. Could not make it work untill i found your post.

replies.author: meowflying
replies.body:
By the way is it normal reply from this server: 

 Disable initial balance range in tpo indicator settings                                                        

● tradingview - indicator\_set\_inputs (MCP)(entity\_id: "dwOqGS", inputs: "{\\"showIB\\": false}")

● tradingview - indicator\_set\_inputs (MCP)(entity\_id: "dwOqGS", inputs: "{\\"showInitialBalance\\": false, \\"ibRange\\": false, \\"initialBalance\\": false, \\"showIbRange\\": false}")

 ● None of those keys are being accepted — the initial balance range setting isn't exposed via the API for the built-in  TPO. You'll need to do it manually: double-click the TPO indicator on the chart → find "Initial Balance" in the   settings and uncheck it.    



It even could not draw SMA 100 with same reply.  But i was able to draw SMA50 without problem when connected to Openrouter free models before that. Hmmm

replies.author: DAlphaOne
replies.body:
Yeah, that's a known limitation with the current TradingView MCP setup (as of April 2026).
The built-in TPO indicator has some settings (especially Initial Balance / IB Range) that simply aren't exposed through the indicator_set_inputs API yet. Claude keeps trying different key names (showIB, initialBalance, ibRange, etc.), but the server correctly tells you none of them work.

Quick fix:
Manually disable it — Double-click the TPO indicator on your chart → go to the Style tab → uncheck "Initial Balance" (or "Initial Balance Range"). That should clean it up immediately.

Same thing sometimes happens with certain settings on other built-in indicators (SMA 100 can be flaky too, while SMA 50 usually works fine). It's not your setup or the guide, it's just a current gap in what MCP can control on TradingView's native indicators.

replies.author: meowflying
replies.body: My Claude cant detect different instruments in Tradingview tabs. Like I have 6 tabs, all different instrument charts, but Claude say all 6 are same instrument. That what he get from the "API". Any idea?

replies.author: DAlphaOne
replies.body: I haven't tried different tabs, only one! It does seem to be able to switch symbols properly though 🤔

comments.author: Better_Jump6098
comments.body:
You can use webhooks. TradingView sends the alert via webhook URL, and an Expert Advisor on MT5 receives it and executes. The setup is around 5 minutes, no coding needed.



I recently made a Spanish tutorial walking through the exact steps if you want to see the process: \[LINK\_VIDEO\] (works the same in English or any language since MT5 UI is visual).



Key things to watch for:

\- MT5 needs "Allow WebRequest" enabled in Tools > Options > Expert Advisors, with your backend URL whitelisted

\- Alert message must be a valid JSON payload

\- AutoTrading button must be green in MT5



Happy to answer follow-ups.

comments.author: Formal-Ear9034
comments.body:
Nibbles is a great name and honestly the MSIX workaround is the part that would have broken me. The COM API approach to force the debug port is not something most people would figure out — fair amount of pain in that writeup.

The chart reading and Pine Script side of this is genuinely useful once it's running. Where I found the ceiling was when I wanted to go from "Claude can see my chart" to "Claude can build and properly backtest a strategy" — TradingView's backtester just doesn't give you enough to trust something with real money.

For that part I've been using Arrow Algo's MCP instead. No Pine Script involved — Claude builds strategies through a visual block builder, and the backtesting is a lot more thorough. Different use case to what you've built here but worth knowing about if Nibbles ever outgrows the analysis side.

Gonna share this with a few people who've been asking about the Windows setup specifically — the MSIX issue trips everyone up.

comments.author: rabihmh
comments.body:
Does this TradingView MCP support running backtests for a Pine Script strategy and returning the results?

I built a similar tool for MetaTrader 5, where it can compile .mq5 code into an .ex5, run backtests, perform walk-forward testing, and return the results automatically.

Now I’m thinking about moving some of my work to TradingView, so I’m looking for a tool that can help with this workflow, or something I can use as a base to continue development on.

Has anyone tested this, or knows if TradingView MCP supports strategy execution/backtesting?
